title: Practice Set FS3 — Repartition the 15TB Unified Meter Table
description: Design partitioning for GridUnion's 15TB unified meter table with 40+ consumers, choose target file sizes from a multi-factor trade-off, review the proposed Z-ORDER strategy, plan the live migration from date-only to country/date partitioning under a 90-day regulatory deadline, and design GridUnion's complete partition governance framework. The flagship task in this set (Task 4) encodes the FS3 Lesson 8 live-table repartition migration that is the single most-detailed lesson in the senior partition module.
version: authored 2026-04-09, targets Microsoft Fabric GA 2025+ / Delta Lake 3.0+

metadata.scenarioScale: 5M meters, 30 regions, 4 countries (DE/FR/NL/LU), 12 teams, 80 engineers, 500+ pipelines, 4 capacities, 15TB historical archive, 5M new rows/day
metadata.calibrationNotes: Senior practice set 3. Most complex set in the scaling phase because of Task 4 (the flagship live repartition migration plan). Two trade-off tasks anchor the partition column choice (Task 1) and file sizing decision (Task 2), one design review tests Z-ORDER strategy critique (Task 3), the design artifact task is the migration plan (Task 4), and the synthesis is governance framework (Task 5). One optional dataset (file distribution sample) supports Task 4's file count math. Multiple defensible answers documented per section 6.3 in F1, F2, and F4.

datasets.description: Sample of the current file distribution in GridUnion's date-only partitioned readings_silver table. 150 files representing 30 days × 5 files per day, totaling 53.6 GB at sample scale. Median file size 279 MB (target band: 256 MB - 1 GB). 23 small files (under 128 MB) suggest compaction lag. 20 oversized files (over 512 MB) suggest skewed writes. The sample mirrors the production 15TB table's distribution shape, scaled down ~280x. Used by Task 4 to inform the migration plan with realistic file count math: if the table is repartitioned to country/date without compaction, the file count multiplies by ~4 (one set per country) and the median drops to ~70 MB — well below the target band.

tasks.title: Select Partition Columns for the Unified Meter Table

description.context:
GridUnion's unified readings_silver table is 15TB with 40+ downstream consumers across 4 countries and 12 teams. The current partitioning is date-only (one partition per day). After the multi-country acquisition, the German regulator (BNetzA) introduced a requirement that all German meter data must be physically separable for audit, and the French regulatory team needs to filter the table by country in nearly every query. The platform team is choosing a new partition strategy.

  CONSUMER QUERY PATTERN SURVEY (top 10 consumers by query frequency):

    Consumer                  Filter pattern                 Daily query count
    --------                  --------------                 -----------------
    German billing pipeline   country='DE' AND date=...      24,000
    French regulatory report  country='FR' AND date=...       18,000
    Dutch operations          country='NL' AND date=...       15,000
    Lux compliance            country='LU' AND date=...        8,000
    Cross-country aggregate   date=...                         6,000
    ML training (DE)          country='DE' AND date>=...       4,500
    ML training (FR)          country='FR' AND date>=...       4,200
    Audit query (BNetzA)      country='DE' AND date>=...       2,400
    Audit query (CRE)         country='FR' AND date>=...       2,100
    Power BI semantic model   date=...                         1,800

  THREE OPTIONS UNDER CONSIDERATION:

    OPTION A — DATE-ONLY (current): partition by date
      - Preserves the cross-country aggregate query (date filter prunes correctly)
      - 9 of 10 top consumers also need country filter; without country in the partition, they read all 4 countries' data and filter at runtime
      - Daily partition count: 30 days × 1 = 30 active partitions (manageable)
      - File count over 3 years: ~5,500 files (well under metadata threshold)
      - Does not satisfy the BNetzA physical separability requirement

    OPTION B — COUNTRY/DATE: partition by (country_code, date)
      - 9 of 10 top consumers prune correctly to a single country
      - Cross-country aggregate query reads all 4 country partitions for the date — slightly more work but acceptable at 6,000 daily queries
      - Daily partition count: 30 days × 4 countries = 120 active partitions
      - File count over 3 years: ~22,000 files (still under metadata threshold)
      - Satisfies BNetzA physical separability

    OPTION C — COUNTRY/REGION/DATE: partition by (country_code, region_code, date)
      - All 30 regions prune independently — German regional billing queries are even faster
      - Daily partition count: 30 days × 30 regions = 900 active partitions
      - File count over 3 years: ~165,000 files (approaches OneLake metadata overhead concerns)
      - Most regions have 1-2 files per day, leading to 'small files' warnings
      - Satisfies BNetzA physical separability AND provides region-level isolation

  GRIDUNION CONSTRAINTS:
    - 40+ downstream consumers must continue to work after the migration
    - 90-day regulatory deadline for BNetzA physical separability
    - F128 production capacity
    - Cross-country aggregate query is the only one that does NOT filter by country
description.task: Evaluate the three partition strategies against GridUnion's constraints and recommend ONE primary approach. Multiple defensible answers exist; reasoning fields determine acceptance.
description.validationHint: Option B (country/date) is the primary defensible answer because it: (1) satisfies the regulatory requirement, (2) accelerates 9 of 10 top consumers, (3) keeps file count manageable, (4) only marginally degrades the cross-country aggregate (one extra partition scan per query at 6K/day is acceptable). Option C is also defensible if the team values region-level isolation but the file count concern is real. Option A is rejected because it does not satisfy the BNetzA requirement and forces 9 of 10 consumers to read all countries' data. Acceptable target partition count range: 100-150 active daily partitions for Option B.

fields.label: RECOMMENDATION: Which partition strategy do you recommend as primary?
options: Option A (Date-only) — minimal change, file count is lowest
Option B (Country/Date) — satisfies BNetzA, accelerates 9 of 10 consumers, file count is manageable
Option C (Country/Region/Date) — maximum granularity, region-level isolation
Hybrid: country/date for 28 days of hot data, country only for older partitions
fields.correctAnswer: Option B (Country/Date) — satisfies BNetzA, accelerates 9 of 10 consumers, file count is manageable
fields.secondaryCorrectAnswer: Option C (Country/Region/Date) — maximum granularity, region-level isolation
fields.rationale: PRIMARY answer is Option B because it satisfies the regulatory requirement, accelerates the dominant query pattern (9 of 10 top consumers filter by country), and keeps file count well under the OneLake metadata threshold. Option C is also defensible if the student values region-level isolation enough to accept the 165K file count and the small-files risk. A staff engineer would defend either, with Option B being the more conservative choice. Option A is rejected because it fails the BNetzA requirement (a hard constraint, not a preference). The Hybrid is rejected as primary because it adds operational complexity without meaningful benefit at the 30-day partition retention.
fields.acceptable_alternatives: Either Option B (primary) or Option C (secondary) is accepted with appropriate reasoning. Option A is NOT accepted under any reasoning because it fails the regulatory hard constraint.

fields.label: TARGET PARTITION COUNT: How many active daily partitions does your chosen strategy produce?
fields.rationale: For Option B (primary): 30 days × 4 countries = 120. For Option C (secondary): 30 days × 30 regions = 900. The wide tolerance accommodates both: a student who picked Option B answers ~120, a student who picked Option C answers ~900 (which is outside the ±30 tolerance and would normally fail). The grading platform must accept the value that matches the student's F1 selection: 90-150 for Option B, 800-1000 for Option C, 30 for Option A.
fields.acceptable_alternatives: Numeric ranges by F1 selection: Option A: 30 (±5). Option B: 90-150. Option C: 800-1000. Hybrid: 60-120.

fields.label: TRADE-OFF FACTORS: Which factors does your chosen approach prioritize?
options: Regulatory compliance with BNetzA physical separability
Query acceleration for the 9 country-filtered consumers
File count manageability
Region-level isolation (granularity)
Operational simplicity (fewest moving parts)
Minimum migration effort (smallest change from current)
correctAnswer: Regulatory compliance with BNetzA physical separability
Query acceleration for the 9 country-filtered consumers
fields.rationale: For Option B: regulatory compliance + query acceleration + file count manageability are the three priorities. For Option C: add 'region-level isolation' to that set. The grading platform must accept the multi-select that matches the student's F1 selection. A student who picked Option A would correctly choose 'operational simplicity' and 'minimum migration effort' but loses on regulatory compliance — and the F1 answer is wrong.
fields.acceptable_alternatives: If F1 = Option C, accept the union including 'Region-level isolation'. The factors must be consistent with the recommended option.

fields.label: EXPIRATION CONDITION: Under what future condition would your recommended strategy stop being right?
options: If the cross-country aggregate query frequency grows from 6,000 to 60,000+ per day, or if a new use case requires frequent unfiltered scans across all countries, the country-first partitioning would become a bottleneck and a date-first or hybrid scheme would need consideration
Never — Option B is permanently correct
When a fifth country is added to GridUnion
When the table size exceeds 30 TB
fields.correctAnswer: If the cross-country aggregate query frequency grows from 6,000 to 60,000+ per day, or if a new use case requires frequent unfiltered scans across all countries, the country-first partitioning would become a bottleneck and a date-first or hybrid scheme would need consideration
fields.rationale: Senior architectural choices have expiration conditions. Option B is right NOW because country-filtered queries dominate (66,500 country-filtered vs 7,800 country-unfiltered per day). If that ratio inverts, the right choice changes. The student must articulate the SPECIFIC condition that would trigger reconsideration, not just say 'when things change.'

fields.label: STRONGEST COUNTERARGUMENT: What is the strongest argument AGAINST your chosen approach that a peer reviewer might raise?
options: The cross-country aggregate query (6,000 daily) becomes slightly less efficient because it must scan 4 country partitions instead of 1 unified partition per date — a 4x increase in partition listing for that query path
Partitioning is unnecessary at 15 TB
The migration is too risky to attempt
There is no counterargument
fields.correctAnswer: The cross-country aggregate query (6,000 daily) becomes slightly less efficient because it must scan 4 country partitions instead of 1 unified partition per date — a 4x increase in partition listing for that query path
fields.rationale: A senior engineer can articulate the strongest peer-review counterargument to their own choice. The cross-country aggregate query DOES get slower with country/date partitioning — that is the real cost of optimizing for the dominant query pattern. The student's defense is that 6,000 queries per day with a small partition listing overhead is acceptable when 66,500 queries per day get faster. Distractor D is wrong because senior engineering requires knowing the trade-off, not denying it exists.

description.context:
After choosing country/date partitioning in Task 1, GridUnion must choose a target file size for the new partitioning scheme. The team is evaluating three target sizes against the 15 TB table that grows 5M rows / 15 GB per day.

  CURRENT STATE (date-only partitioning):
    Total size:        15 TB
    File count:        ~5,500 files
    Median file size:  280 MB
    Largest file:      1.4 GB
    Smallest file:     38 MB
    Daily growth:      15 GB / day across the 4 countries

  AFTER COUNTRY/DATE MIGRATION (without compaction):
    Total size:        15 TB (same data, different layout)
    File count:        ~22,000 files (4x — one set per country per date)
    Median file size:  ~70 MB (small files problem)

  THREE TARGET FILE SIZE OPTIONS:

    OPTION A — 256 MB target:
      - Final file count after compaction: ~60,000 files (4 countries × 30 days × ~500 files per partition)
      - Read efficiency: best for selective queries (single date, single region within country)
      - Write efficiency: more files per write batch, more metadata operations
      - OneLake metadata overhead: significant at 60K files

    OPTION B — 512 MB target:
      - Final file count after compaction: ~30,000 files
      - Read efficiency: balanced — good for both selective and broad scans
      - Write efficiency: fewer files per batch, less metadata churn
      - OneLake metadata overhead: moderate

    OPTION C — 1 GB target:
      - Final file count after compaction: ~15,000 files
      - Read efficiency: best for broad scans (entire month, full country)
      - Write efficiency: highest (fewest files per batch)
      - OneLake metadata overhead: minimal
      - Risk: at 1 GB per file, a single Spark task processes one file end-to-end; if a query touches 5 files, only 5 tasks can run in parallel — limits parallelism for selective queries

  GRIDUNION QUERY MIX:
    Selective (single date, single region):  ~60% of queries
    Date range (single country):              ~30% of queries  
    Broad scans (entire country, multi-month): ~10% of queries (analyst exports, ML training, regulatory)
description.task: Choose a target file size and document the reasoning. Multiple defensible answers exist within the 256 MB - 1 GB range.
description.validationHint: Option B (512 MB) is the canonical balanced answer and matches the FS3 Lesson 2 recommendation for multi-tenant tables with mixed query patterns. Option A (256 MB) is also defensible if the student emphasizes selective query performance. Option C (1 GB) is defensible for write-optimized scenarios but the parallelism limit for selective queries (60% of the workload) is the strongest counterargument. The numeric tolerance accepts any value in the 256-1024 range with appropriate reasoning.

fields.label: RECOMMENDATION: Which target file size do you recommend?
options: Option A (256 MB) — optimizes for the 60% selective queries, more parallelism, smaller per-task work
Option B (512 MB) — balanced across query types, manageable file count, moderate metadata overhead
Option C (1 GB) — fewest files, lowest metadata overhead, optimizes for the 10% broad scans
Mixed: 256 MB for hot partitions (last 30 days), 512 MB for warm (30-180 days), 1 GB for cold (180+ days)
fields.correctAnswer: Option B (512 MB) — balanced across query types, manageable file count, moderate metadata overhead
fields.secondaryCorrectAnswer: Option A (256 MB) — optimizes for the 60% selective queries, more parallelism, smaller per-task work
fields.rationale: PRIMARY answer is Option B because it balances all 3 query patterns and matches the FS3 Lesson 2 standard for mixed-workload tables. SECONDARY answer is Option A because the 60% selective query majority is a defensible reason to optimize for parallelism. Option C is rejected because it sacrifices selective query parallelism (60% of workload) for broad scan optimization (10% of workload). The Mixed approach is technically optimal but adds operational complexity that exceeds the benefit at GridUnion's scale.
fields.acceptable_alternatives: Either Option B (primary) or Option A (secondary) is accepted. Option C accepted only with explicit reasoning addressing the parallelism limit. Mixed accepted only with explicit reasoning addressing the operational complexity of tiered file sizes.

fields.label: TARGET SIZE IN MB: What target file size do you recommend? (Must match your F1 selection — answer in MB.)
fields.rationale: Tolerance ±256 MB accepts the range 256 MB - 768 MB, which covers Option A (256), Option B (512), and the lower bound of compromise answers. Option C (1024 MB) is outside this tolerance and the student would need to defend the choice in F1 with appropriate reasoning. The grading platform must accept the value that matches the student's F1 selection.
fields.acceptable_alternatives: Numeric ranges by F1 selection: Option A: 256 (±50). Option B: 512 (±100). Option C: 1024 (±100). Mixed: any value in 256-1024 with note.

fields.label: TRADE-OFF: What is the primary cost of your chosen file size?
options: Option B's 30,000 file count is moderate metadata overhead — not as efficient as Option C's 15,000 but not as bad as Option A's 60,000
Option B is slower than Option A for selective queries because tasks process more data per file
Option B has no costs
Option B is more expensive than Option C in storage
fields.correctAnswer: Option B's 30,000 file count is moderate metadata overhead — not as efficient as Option C's 15,000 but not as bad as Option A's 60,000
fields.rationale: Every architectural choice has costs. Option B's cost is the moderate metadata overhead (still better than Option A's 60K but worse than Option C's 15K). The student must KNOW the cost they accepted, not deny it exists. If the student picked Option A, the correct answer would emphasize the higher metadata overhead. The grading platform must accept the cost matching F1.
fields.acceptable_alternatives: If F1 = Option A, the cost is 'higher metadata overhead from 60K files'. If F1 = Option C, the cost is 'limited parallelism for the 60% selective queries because each task processes 1 GB end-to-end'.

fields.label: EXPIRATION CONDITION: Under what condition would your chosen file size stop being right?
options: If the query mix shifts from 60% selective to 60% broad scans (e.g., NordGrid acquires a competitor and the analyst team's daily exports become the dominant workload), the file size should grow to optimize for the new dominant pattern
Never — file size is permanent
When a 5th country joins
When OneLake adds compression
fields.correctAnswer: If the query mix shifts from 60% selective to 60% broad scans (e.g., NordGrid acquires a competitor and the analyst team's daily exports become the dominant workload), the file size should grow to optimize for the new dominant pattern
fields.rationale: The right file size depends on the query mix. The choice expires when the mix changes substantially. Senior reasoning includes the SPECIFIC condition (workload shift) and the expected response (re-tune file size to match the new dominant pattern).

description.context:
GridUnion's platform team proposed the following Z-ORDER strategy for the new country/date partitioned readings_silver table. A senior engineer is reviewing it before deployment.

  PROPOSED Z-ORDER CONFIGURATION:

    Table:        readings_silver
    Partitioning: country_code, date  (from Task 1)
    File size:    512 MB target  (from Task 2)

    Z-ORDER columns: (country_code, region_code, customer_id, meter_id)
    Refresh cadence: weekly (Sunday 02:00 UTC)
    OPTIMIZE strategy: ZORDER BY (country_code, region_code, customer_id, meter_id)

  DOWNSTREAM CONSUMER QUERY PATTERNS (from Task 1):

    Most queries:           WHERE country_code = ? AND date = ?
    Some queries:           WHERE country_code = ? AND date BETWEEN ? AND ?
    Some queries:           WHERE country_code = ? AND region_code IN (?, ?)
    Some queries:           WHERE meter_id = ? (point lookup, ~50/day)
    Some queries:           WHERE customer_id = ? (point lookup, ~30/day)
    Cross-country aggregate: WHERE date = ? (no country filter)

  PROPOSED OPTIMIZE COMMAND:

    OPTIMIZE readings_silver
    WHERE date >= current_date() - INTERVAL 7 DAYS
    ZORDER BY (country_code, region_code, customer_id, meter_id)

  PLATFORM TEAM'S REASONING (from the proposal):
    'We chose 4 Z-ORDER columns to cover all query patterns. The weekly cadence keeps maintenance cost low. The OPTIMIZE only touches the last 7 days of partitions to avoid expensive full-table operations.'
description.task: Identify the 4 problems with this Z-ORDER strategy. Each problem should reflect a real anti-pattern that a senior reviewer would block on. Severity-rank each.
description.validationHint: The 4 problems are: (1) country_code is in the Z-ORDER list AND is a partition column — Z-ORDER on a partition column wastes effort because partition pruning already isolates data by country. (2) Four Z-ORDER columns is too many — Z-ORDER's effectiveness drops sharply past 2-3 columns because the space-filling curve becomes diluted. (3) The OPTIMIZE only touches 7 days of partitions, but Z-ORDER must run on the partition being optimized — running ZORDER on already-Z-ORDERED partitions repeatedly is wasteful, while older partitions never get Z-ORDERED at all. (4) The point lookup queries by meter_id and customer_id are too rare (~80/day) to justify Z-ORDERing on those columns — the optimization cost outweighs the benefit for such low query frequency.

fields.label: PROBLEM 1: Which is the most severe issue?
options: country_code is BOTH a partition column AND a Z-ORDER column — Z-ORDER on a partition column is wasted effort because partition pruning already isolates data by country before Z-ORDER's row-group skipping can apply. country_code should be REMOVED from the Z-ORDER list
The weekly cadence is too infrequent
The OPTIMIZE WHERE clause is wrong
ZORDER syntax is incorrect
fields.correctAnswer: country_code is BOTH a partition column AND a Z-ORDER column — Z-ORDER on a partition column is wasted effort because partition pruning already isolates data by country before Z-ORDER's row-group skipping can apply. country_code should be REMOVED from the Z-ORDER list
fields.rationale: This is the textbook Z-ORDER mistake at senior level: Z-ORDER complements partition pruning, it does not duplicate it. Including a partition column in Z-ORDER means the space-filling curve wastes a dimension on data that is already physically separated. The 4-column Z-ORDER becomes effectively a 3-column Z-ORDER with one wasted dimension, weakening the clustering for the remaining columns. Senior reviewers catch this consistently; mid-level engineers usually do not.

fields.label: PROBLEM 2: What is the next-most severe problem?
options: Four Z-ORDER columns is too many — Z-ORDER's effectiveness drops sharply past 2-3 columns because the space-filling curve dimensions are diluted. The 4-column proposal should be reduced to 2 columns, optimally (region_code, date) since date is already a partition but region_code is the most-used filter after country
The weekly cadence is wrong
OPTIMIZE should run daily
Z-ORDER is not supported in Fabric
fields.correctAnswer: Four Z-ORDER columns is too many — Z-ORDER's effectiveness drops sharply past 2-3 columns because the space-filling curve dimensions are diluted. The 4-column proposal should be reduced to 2 columns, optimally (region_code, date) since date is already a partition but region_code is the most-used filter after country
fields.rationale: Z-ORDER's underlying space-filling curve loses effectiveness past 2-3 dimensions because the bit interleaving distributes the clustering benefit across too many axes. Senior teams typically Z-ORDER on 1-2 columns max. The 4-column proposal is a common mid-level mistake of 'cover all query patterns' which actually weakens clustering for every individual pattern.

fields.label: PROBLEM 3: What is the third-most severe issue?
options: The OPTIMIZE WHERE clause only Z-ORDERs the last 7 days of partitions, but: (a) those partitions are already Z-ORDERED from the previous week, so the operation is redundant on the older 6 days, AND (b) older partitions (8+ days) never get Z-ORDERED at all, so they degrade over time. The right pattern is OPTIMIZE on partitions whose Z-ORDER STATE is stale, not on partitions matching a date range
OPTIMIZE should run on the entire table
OPTIMIZE should be disabled
OPTIMIZE should run hourly
fields.correctAnswer: The OPTIMIZE WHERE clause only Z-ORDERs the last 7 days of partitions, but: (a) those partitions are already Z-ORDERED from the previous week, so the operation is redundant on the older 6 days, AND (b) older partitions (8+ days) never get Z-ORDERED at all, so they degrade over time. The right pattern is OPTIMIZE on partitions whose Z-ORDER STATE is stale, not on partitions matching a date range
fields.rationale: Z-ORDER maintenance at scale requires tracking which partitions have been Z-ORDERED and which have changed since their last Z-ORDER. A blind 'last 7 days' filter is wasteful AND incomplete: wasteful because most of those partitions were already Z-ORDERED last week, incomplete because partitions that received late-arriving data after their initial Z-ORDER never get re-clustered. The senior pattern is to track Z-ORDER state in a metadata table and selectively re-optimize only partitions where the state is stale.

fields.label: PROBLEM 4: What is the fourth issue, and what makes it least severe?
options: Including customer_id and meter_id in Z-ORDER for ~80 daily point-lookup queries does not justify the optimization cost — those columns could be removed without measurable query slowdown. Least severe because the existence of the columns in Z-ORDER mainly wastes effort during OPTIMIZE rather than degrading queries
The cadence should be hourly
The table should not be Z-ORDERED
OPTIMIZE should be disabled
fields.correctAnswer: Including customer_id and meter_id in Z-ORDER for ~80 daily point-lookup queries does not justify the optimization cost — those columns could be removed without measurable query slowdown. Least severe because the existence of the columns in Z-ORDER mainly wastes effort during OPTIMIZE rather than degrading queries
fields.rationale: Z-ORDER optimization for low-frequency query patterns is a cost vs benefit miscalibration. 80 daily point lookups against a 15 TB table do not justify Z-ORDERing on customer_id and meter_id — the OPTIMIZE cost across 22,000 files exceeds the cumulative query speedup. This is the least severe of the 4 issues because it 'only' wastes maintenance CUs, where the other 3 actively degrade query performance or coverage.

fields.label: REVISED RECOMMENDATION: What Z-ORDER configuration should the reviewer recommend?
options: ZORDER BY (region_code) only — region_code is the most-used filter after country (which is partitioned), single-column Z-ORDER preserves clustering effectiveness, and the 80 daily point lookups can use Bloom filters or be accepted as full scans within their already-pruned partition
ZORDER BY (country_code, region_code, date)
ZORDER BY all 4 original columns
Disable Z-ORDER entirely
fields.correctAnswer: ZORDER BY (region_code) only — region_code is the most-used filter after country (which is partitioned), single-column Z-ORDER preserves clustering effectiveness, and the 80 daily point lookups can use Bloom filters or be accepted as full scans within their already-pruned partition
fields.secondaryCorrectAnswer: ZORDER BY (region_code, customer_id) — region_code as the primary cluster key, customer_id as a secondary because point-lookup queries appear in the survey
fields.rationale: PRIMARY answer is single-column Z-ORDER on region_code — the senior pattern for tables where one column dominates the non-partition filter pattern. SECONDARY answer (region_code + customer_id) is also defensible if the team values the point-lookup queries enough to justify a 2-column Z-ORDER. Both maintain the principle: never Z-ORDER on a partition column, never exceed 2-3 columns, justify each column with query frequency.
fields.acceptable_alternatives: Either single-column (region_code) or two-column (region_code + customer_id) Z-ORDER is accepted. Three-column or four-column proposals are rejected.

tasks.title: Plan the Live Repartition Migration

description.context:
This is the FLAGSHIP TASK of the FS3 module. GridUnion must migrate the readings_silver table from date-only partitioning to country/date partitioning under a 90-day BNetzA regulatory deadline. The table is 15 TB, has 40+ downstream consumers, and cannot have any downtime — the morning analyst window must continue every business day during the migration.

  CURRENT STATE:
    Table:           readings_silver
    Size:            15 TB
    Partitioning:    date (one partition per day)
    File count:      ~5,500 files
    Consumers:       40+ pipelines and reports across 4 countries
    Daily writes:    15 GB / day from the Bronze layer
    Daily reads:     ~80,000 queries/day across all consumers

  TARGET STATE:
    Same table (must be the SAME table from the consumers' perspective — no rename)
    Partitioning:    (country_code, date)
    Target file size: 512 MB
    Z-ORDER:         (region_code) — from Task 3
    File count:      ~30,000 files after compaction

  CONSTRAINT:
    Zero downtime: morning analyst window (07:00-12:00 UTC) must work every business day during the migration
    Rollback ready: any phase must be reversible if it causes a regression
    BNetzA deadline: 90 days from start

  THREE MIGRATION APPROACHES (the FS3 Lesson 8 trade-off):

    APPROACH 1 — DUAL-WRITE: Write to both old and new tables during a transition period; consumers gradually migrate; old table is dropped when no consumers remain
      Pros: zero risk to existing consumers, gradual rollout, easy rollback
      Cons: 2x storage during transition, complex pipeline modifications, requires schema discipline

    APPROACH 2 — VIEW ABSTRACTION: Rename the existing table, create a new table with new partitioning, create a view that points consumers to the new table; old data is migrated in batches behind the view
      Pros: consumers see no change, gradual data migration, view layer enables rollback
      Cons: view performance overhead, complex view definition, potential for view to drift from underlying tables

    APPROACH 3 — DELTA CRAS (Create Replace As Select): Use Delta's CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE to atomically rewrite the table with new partitioning in a single operation
      Pros: atomic switch, no transition period, simplest mental model
      Cons: requires processing all 15 TB in one operation (20+ hours of CU), no rollback once committed, blocks consumers during the operation
description.task:
Build a Python dict called migration_plan with these top-level keys:
  - approach: one of 'dual_write', 'view_abstraction', or 'delta_cras'
  - rationale: a non-empty string explaining why this approach was chosen
  - phases: a list of phase dicts (4-7 phases). Each phase has order (int), name (string), description (string), duration_days (int), reversible (bool)
  - rollback_plan: a string describing how to roll back if a regression is detected mid-migration
  - file_count_before: the current file count (5500)
  - file_count_after_target: the post-migration target file count (30000, from the Task 2 file size analysis)
  - consumer_notification_days_before: integer days of notice given to consumers before any breaking change (typically 30+ for senior governance)
  - regulatory_deadline_days: 90
  - total_duration_days: integer total — must be less than 90
description.validationHint: DUAL-WRITE is the senior canonical answer for this scenario because it satisfies the zero-downtime constraint AND the rollback constraint AND fits within 90 days. VIEW_ABSTRACTION is also defensible. DELTA_CRAS is rejected because it cannot satisfy zero downtime (the rewrite blocks consumers) and cannot be rolled back. The phases list typically includes: prepare new table, enable dual-write, verify new table, migrate consumers, decommission old table. Phases 1-4 should be reversible (the rollback constraint); phase 5 (decommission) is the irreversible one. consumer_notification_days_before should be at least 30 (NordGrid mid-level standard) and ideally 60+ for senior governance.

starterScaffold.content:
# FS3 Lesson 8: live table repartitioning migration plan.
# This is the FLAGSHIP task — the most detailed migration design in the senior track.

# YOUR SOLUTION BELOW
# Build migration_plan with all 9 required keys from the task description.
# The phases list should have 4-7 phase dicts in order.

migration_plan = {
    # Fill in all 9 keys
}

# YOUR SOLUTION ABOVE

# Verification (do not modify)
import json
print(json.dumps(migration_plan, indent=2))

assertions.description: migration_plan has all 9 required top-level keys
approach is one of the three documented options
approach is NOT delta_cras (rejected because it cannot satisfy zero-downtime AND no-rollback constraints)
phases is a list of 4-7 entries
every phase has the 5 required keys
phase order values are sequential starting from 1
assertions.check: [p['order'] for p in migration_plan['phases']] == list(range(1, len(migration_plan['phases']) + 1))

assertions.description: at least 3 phases are marked reversible (the rollback constraint requires most phases to be undoable)
assertions.check: sum(1 for p in migration_plan['phases'] if p['reversible']) >= 3

assertions.description: file_count_before equals 5500 and file_count_after_target equals 30000 (from Tasks 1-2)
consumer_notification_days_before is at least 30 days (NordGrid mid-level standard, often more at senior)
total_duration_days is less than 90 (must fit within the regulatory deadline)
rollback_plan is a non-empty string explaining how mid-migration rollback works

tasks.partialCredit: 12 independent assertions. Each passed assertion earns 1/12 credit. A3 is the keystone — it explicitly rejects delta_cras as a valid approach for this scenario, catching students who picked the operationally simplest option without considering the zero-downtime and rollback constraints.

tasks.title: GridUnion's Partition Governance Framework

description.context:
This is the synthesis task. GridUnion's platform team needs an ongoing partition governance framework to ensure that the Task 4 migration is not the last partition decision the team makes. New tables get created weekly across 12 teams, and without governance every team will make its own partition choices — leading to the same kind of regulatory and performance problems that drove the current migration.

Your job has two parts: (1) DIAGNOSIS — fill the 4-field template articulating the governance principles. (2) ARTIFACT — build a Python dict specifying the framework's components, processes, and policies.
description.task:
Fill the diagnosis template AND build the partition_governance dict.

For the ARTIFACT: Build a Python dict called partition_governance with these keys:
  - design_review_required_for: a list containing the table types that must go through partition design review. Must include 'fact_tables_above_100gb', 'multi_consumer_tables', 'regulatory_tables'
  - review_committee: a dict with members (list of role names), meeting_cadence ('weekly'), escalation_path (string)
  - monitoring_metrics: a list of EXACTLY 4 metric names: 'file_count_per_table', 'median_file_size', 'partition_count_per_table', 'small_files_percentage'
  - alert_thresholds: a dict with file_count_warning (10000), file_count_critical (100000), small_files_pct_warning (15)
  - quarterly_audit_enabled: True
  - migration_playbook_documented: True
  - approved_partition_patterns: a list containing at least 'date_only', 'country_date', 'country_region_date'
description.validationHint: The synthesis combines partition column selection (Task 1), file sizing (Task 2), Z-ORDER strategy (Task 3), and migration planning (Task 4) into a governance framework that prevents future ad-hoc partition decisions. The 4 monitoring metrics are the same ones the platform team will track after the Task 4 migration completes.

fields.label: DIAGNOSIS 1: Why does partition design need a formal review process at GridUnion's scale?
options: Without governance, individual teams make partition choices that work for their immediate query patterns but conflict with cross-team consumers, regulatory requirements, or capacity costs. The Task 1 scenario (40+ consumers across 4 countries) shows that no single team has the full picture; only a cross-team review can balance the trade-offs
Microsoft Fabric requires partition reviews
Reviews are bureaucratic but mandatory
Individual engineers make better decisions than committees
fields.correctAnswer: Without governance, individual teams make partition choices that work for their immediate query patterns but conflict with cross-team consumers, regulatory requirements, or capacity costs. The Task 1 scenario (40+ consumers across 4 countries) shows that no single team has the full picture; only a cross-team review can balance the trade-offs
fields.rationale: Senior governance exists where individual decisions have multi-team consequences. Partition strategy is a perfect example: the German billing team's 'best' partitioning differs from the French regulatory team's 'best' partitioning, and both differ from the cross-country aggregate's 'best'. Only a forum that includes all consumers can choose the right balance. This is the FS1 cross-team ownership contract pattern applied to partition decisions.

fields.label: DIAGNOSIS 2: Why does the framework require monitoring metrics in addition to the design review?
options: Design reviews catch problems at table creation, but partitioning degrades over time as data volumes grow, query patterns shift, and small files accumulate. Without ongoing monitoring, a table that was perfectly designed in 2025 becomes a performance problem in 2027 and nobody notices until consumers complain
Monitoring is required by regulation
Reviews are unreliable
Monitoring replaces reviews
fields.correctAnswer: Design reviews catch problems at table creation, but partitioning degrades over time as data volumes grow, query patterns shift, and small files accumulate. Without ongoing monitoring, a table that was perfectly designed in 2025 becomes a performance problem in 2027 and nobody notices until consumers complain
fields.rationale: The same prevention-vs-detection duality from FI7 and FS2 applies here: review prevents bad initial decisions, monitoring detects when good initial decisions become bad over time. Both are necessary.

fields.label: DIAGNOSIS 3: Why is the small_files_pct alert threshold set to 15% (warning) instead of 5% or 50%?
options: 15% is the inflection point where small files start to materially affect query performance: below 15% the impact is negligible, above 15% scan time increases noticeably as the optimizer juggles file metadata. 5% would alert constantly on healthy tables; 50% would only alert after performance was already degraded
15% is the Fabric default
15% is required by Delta Lake
It is arbitrary
fields.correctAnswer: 15% is the inflection point where small files start to materially affect query performance: below 15% the impact is negligible, above 15% scan time increases noticeably as the optimizer juggles file metadata. 5% would alert constantly on healthy tables; 50% would only alert after performance was already degraded
fields.rationale: PRIMARY answer reflects the senior knowledge that thresholds have rationale grounded in observed inflection points. SECONDARY answer (arbitrary) is honestly accepted because 10% or 20% would also be defensible — the value is somewhat arbitrary within reasonable bounds but the 15% choice is well-supported.

fields.label: DIAGNOSIS 4: Why does the framework include 'approved_partition_patterns' as a list rather than allowing arbitrary patterns?
options: Constraining partition patterns to a small approved set (date_only, country_date, country_region_date) prevents teams from inventing novel partitioning that the monitoring tools, migration runbooks, and Z-ORDER strategies don't know how to handle. Standardization at scale beats local optimization
Fabric only supports these patterns
Other patterns are illegal
Standardization is preferred but not required
fields.correctAnswer: Constraining partition patterns to a small approved set (date_only, country_date, country_region_date) prevents teams from inventing novel partitioning that the monitoring tools, migration runbooks, and Z-ORDER strategies don't know how to handle. Standardization at scale beats local optimization
fields.rationale: Senior governance trades off some local optimization for platform-wide consistency. A team that invents a clever new partition scheme creates work for everyone else: the monitoring tools must learn to track it, the migration playbook must learn to repartition it, the Z-ORDER strategy must accommodate it. At 200+ tables and 12 teams, this overhead exceeds the local benefit. Standardize on 3-5 patterns that cover 95% of cases and require justification for anything else.

starterScaffold.content:
# FS3 SYNTHESIS: GridUnion's partition governance framework.

# YOUR SOLUTION BELOW
# Build partition_governance with the keys from the task description.

partition_governance = {
    # Fill in all required keys
}

# YOUR SOLUTION ABOVE

# Verification (do not modify)
import json
print(json.dumps(partition_governance, indent=2))

assertions.description: partition_governance has all 7 required top-level keys
design_review_required_for includes all 3 expected table types
review_committee has members list, meeting_cadence='weekly', and escalation_path
assertions.check: 'members' in partition_governance['review_committee'] and partition_governance['review_committee']['meeting_cadence'] == 'weekly' and 'escalation_path' in partition_governance['review_committee']

assertions.description: monitoring_metrics is a list with all 4 required metrics
quarterly_audit_enabled and migration_playbook_documented are both True
approved_partition_patterns includes the 3 patterns from this practice set

tasks.partialCredit: Combined grading: template (4 fields, 50% weight) + assertions (7 code checks, 50% weight). Final score = 0.5 * (template_passed/4) + 0.5 * (assertions_passed/7).

conceptsCombined: Partition column selection from consumer survey (Lesson 1, Task 1)
File sizing trade-off for mixed workloads (Lesson 2, Task 2)
Z-ORDER strategy with column count and partition-column constraints (Lesson 3, Task 3)
Live migration planning with phases and rollback (Lesson 8, Task 4)
Cross-team partition governance with monitoring and approval (Lessons 9-10)
synthesisNotes.rationale: The synthesis combines Tasks 1-4 into a governance framework that prevents future ad-hoc partition decisions. The keystone is the COMBINATION of design review (prevents bad initial decisions) and monitoring (detects degradation over time) — neither alone is sufficient. The approved_partition_patterns list (A7) is the standardization control that prevents team-by-team divergence.

selfCheck.calibrationNotes: Senior practice set 3. Most complex set in the scaling phase. Two trade-off tasks (Tasks 1 and 2) within the section 6.2 max of 3. One design review (Task 3) with 4 subtle Z-ORDER anti-patterns including the senior-level mistake of Z-ORDERing on a partition column. The flagship design artifact (Task 4) is the live repartition migration plan with 12 assertions and an explicit reject of delta_cras as the wrong approach for the constraints. The synthesis (Task 5) builds the governance framework. Multi-defensible answers throughout: partition column choice (Option B or C), file size (Option A or B), Z-ORDER columns (1 or 2), migration approach (dual_write or view_abstraction). The optional dataset supports Task 4's file count math.
selfCheck.theoryPracticeBoundary: The theory teaches partition design for multi-consumer tables (Lesson 1), partition sizing at terabyte scale (Lesson 2), Z-ORDER mechanics (Lesson 3), DPP internals (Lesson 4), small files management (Lesson 5), bucketing (Lesson 6), evolution strategies (Lesson 7), live repartitioning (Lesson 8 — the flagship), monitoring (Lesson 9), and governance (Lesson 10). The practice tests application: Task 1 forces a real cross-consumer trade-off with 40+ consumers and a regulatory hard constraint. Task 2 forces a file size choice with three competing query patterns. Task 3 reviews a Z-ORDER configuration with 4 subtle problems including the partition-column mistake from Lesson 3. Task 4 is the flagship migration plan from Lesson 8 with the 3 approaches and the rejection of delta_cras. Task 5 synthesizes everything into the Lesson 10 governance framework.
selfCheck.datasetNotes: One optional dataset (150 rows of file distribution) supports Task 4's file count math. The dataset is realistic-shape: median 279 MB, 23 small files (under 128 MB), 20 oversized files (over 512 MB). Students can use it to compute the post-migration file count if compaction is included or omitted from the migration plan.
